metadata.description: Historical market reaction patterns for specific news event types. All return figures are averages/medians from historical event studies. Individual outcomes vary significantly based on macro context, degree of market anticipation, and company-specific factors.
metadata.return_convention: All returns are approximate average/median changes from pre-announcement close. Positive = market up; Negative = market down.
metadata.confidence_scale: HIGH=well-documented, multiple studies, consistent pattern | MODERATE=general tendency, high variance | LOW=weak or context-dependent pattern
data_sources: Visual Capitalist / RBC Wealth Management rate-cut analysis
CFI / Wall Street Oasis M&A premium data
Biotech event studies (Journal of Finance and Accountancy; PMC)
J.P. Morgan, T. Rowe Price, State Street institutional research

patterns.name: First Rate Cut in a Federal Reserve Easing Cycle
patterns.description: Initial Fed rate cut after a tightening or neutral period. Market reaction heavily dependent on whether cut accompanies a recession or is a 'soft landing insurance cut'.
patterns.avg_return_1h: +0.3% to +0.8% (if clean dovish cut; flat to negative if hawkish cut)
patterns.fade_signal: Fade (sell) when: Fed signals one-and-done cut; unemployment rising; yield curve not steepening; 'hawkish cut' language used
patterns.follow_signal: Follow (hold/buy) when: clear soft landing; employment strong; inflation already near target; multiple future cuts signaled
patterns.notes: The most important variable is recession probability. Non-recession first cuts have historically been excellent buying opportunities. Recession-accompanied cuts preceded the worst bear markets (2001, 2008). Year 2 of easing cycle with no recession: S&P 500 historically >16% return.

patterns.name: First Rate Hike in a Federal Reserve Tightening Cycle
patterns.description: Initial Fed rate hike from accommodative levels. Short-term headwind but historically bull markets survive entire hiking cycles if the economy remains strong.
patterns.avg_return_1d: Flat to -1.0% (uncertainty removal can cause partial reversal)
patterns.avg_return_30d: -3.0% to -7.0% (since 1999, S&P always down 3 months after first hike on avg)
patterns.avg_return_full_cycle: +62.9% S&P 500 average across full hiking cycles since 1989
patterns.confidence: MODERATE (short-term negative predictable; long-term depends entirely on macro)
patterns.fade_signal: Fade the initial selloff when: economy is clearly strong; earnings growth positive; hike is normalization not emergency tightening
patterns.follow_signal: Follow (stay bearish) when: hike responding to uncontrolled inflation; aggressive pace signaled; yield curve already flat/inverted

sector_rotation.late_cycle_shift: Rotate to quality / low-vol as cycle matures

patterns.notes: 2022 was atypical: S&P fell -25% in the calendar year due to supply-shock inflation requiring very aggressive hikes (+75bps increments). Normal hiking cycles do not produce this. The first hike day itself averages near-flat (+0.03%) with +0.83% average 1-month return.

patterns.name: Large Earnings Beat (EPS >10% Above Consensus)
patterns.description: Company reports earnings per share more than 10% above the Wall Street consensus estimate. Reaction depends heavily on guidance and whether the beat was anticipated.
patterns.avg_return_30d: +5% to +10% (full PEAD drift over 60 trading days; guidance-dependent)
patterns.confidence: HIGH (beat + raised guidance); MODERATE (beat + maintained guidance); LOW (beat + lowered guidance)
patterns.fade_signal: Fade (sell/trim) when: stock rallied >20% in prior 30 days (priced in); beat driven by cost cuts not revenue; guidance only maintained; P/E already 40x+
patterns.follow_signal: Follow (hold/add) when: beat + raised guidance; revenue also beat; stock was flat or down pre-earnings; mid-cap with low analyst coverage (stronger PEAD)
beat_quality_hierarchy: 1. Revenue beat + EPS beat + Guidance raised = STRONGEST (full follow)
2. EPS beat + Revenue beat + Guidance maintained = STRONG (follow)
3. EPS beat only (cost cuts) + Guidance maintained = MODERATE (limited follow)
4. EPS beat + Revenue miss + Guidance maintained = WEAK (fade likely)
5. EPS beat + Guidance cut = NEGATIVE (fade immediately)

pead_parameters.exit_timing: Day before next earnings announcement or at 60-trading-day mark

patterns.notes: ~75% of S&P 500 companies beat EPS in a typical quarter; consensus is set conservatively. This means a simple beat is insufficient — magnitude and quality of beat determine the reaction. Guidance is the most forward-looking and therefore most market-moving component.

patterns.name: Large Earnings Miss (EPS >10% Below Consensus)
patterns.description: Company reports EPS more than 10% below consensus. One of the most reliably negative events in equity markets, especially when combined with guidance cut.
patterns.fade_signal: Fade (buy the dip) when: miss was clearly one-time/non-recurring; guidance raised despite miss; stock already down 30-40% before earnings; sector-wide macro issue (everyone missed)
patterns.follow_signal: Follow (sell/short) when: miss + guidance cut; structural issue identified (competitive share loss); management language is evasive; analyst consensus was already conservative
patterns.worst_case: Miss + guidance cut + CEO evasiveness: average -10% to -20% day; -20% to -30% over 30 days. 'Triple threat' in growth stocks can be -30% to -50%.
patterns.kitchen_sink_exception: New CEO deliberately lowers bar in first/second quarter ('kitchen sink' quarter); buy after the initial selloff as subsequent quarters will likely beat the reset bar
patterns.notes: Negative reactions tend to persist longer and be larger in magnitude than positive reactions of equivalent absolute surprise — consistent with Kahneman prospect theory and loss aversion in markets.

patterns.name: Management Guidance Raised (Full Year or Next Quarter)
patterns.description: Company raises forward earnings/revenue guidance during or after an earnings report. Considered more important than current-quarter EPS in most cases.
patterns.confidence: HIGH — guidance raise is the single most consistently positive catalyst in earnings context
patterns.fade_signal: Fade when: guidance raise is very small (<1-2%) below expectations; raised guidance implies deceleration in growth rate; valuation already stretched (implied by DCF)
patterns.follow_signal: Follow when: guidance raise is >5% above prior guidance; raises across multiple metrics (revenue AND EPS); multiple quarters of consecutive guidance raises (trend matters)

combination_effects.beat_plus_raise: Strongest possible: avg +5% to +8% day; full PEAD follow-through
combination_effects.miss_plus_raise: Unusual but positive: avg +1% to +3% if raise is significant; signals management confidence
combination_effects.beat_plus_maintain: Moderate: avg +2% to +4%; PEAD weaker than beat+raise
combination_effects.beat_plus_cut: Negative despite beat: avg -2% to -5%; market prices future, not past

patterns.notes: Forward guidance is more valuable than current EPS because it tells the market where earnings power is heading. Companies that consistently beat-and-raise create 'beat-and-raise' multiple premiums and sustained PEAD patterns.

patterns.name: Management Guidance Cut (Full Year or Next Quarter)
patterns.description: Company reduces forward earnings/revenue guidance. One of the most reliably negative catalysts, especially if accompanied by an earnings miss.
patterns.confidence: HIGH — guidance cut is the most consistently negative earnings event
patterns.fade_signal: Fade (buy after selloff) when: pre-announcement already happened (double-discount); 'kitchen sink' cut from new management; cut is very conservative and sets low bar; sector-wide issue not company-specific
patterns.follow_signal: Follow (sell/avoid) when: guidance cut in growth stock with high multiple; CEO language is cautious/vague; second consecutive guidance cut; competitive dynamics cited
patterns.pre_announcement_dynamic: If company pre-announces a guidance cut before formal earnings report, stock already sold off on pre-announcement day. Formal earnings release then often trades FLAT or slightly up as the bad news was already absorbed.
patterns.notes: Growth stocks (P/E > 30x) are hit hardest on guidance cuts because valuation depends heavily on out-year earnings assumptions. A 10% guidance cut on a 40x P/E stock justifies more than a 10% stock decline due to valuation multiple compression.

patterns.name: Large-Scale Layoff Announcement (5,000+ Employees)
patterns.description: Company announces layoff of 5,000 or more employees. Market reaction is highly context-dependent — the same absolute number of layoffs is interpreted very differently based on macroeconomic environment and company narrative.
patterns.avg_return_1h: Positive context: +0.5% to +2%. Negative context: -1% to -3%
patterns.avg_return_1d: Average across all types: -1% to -3%. Tech restructuring bull market: +1% to +3%. Recession-signal layoffs: -2% to -5%
patterns.avg_return_30d: Driven by subsequent earnings/guidance — no reliable directional pattern beyond 30 days
patterns.confidence: LOW to MODERATE — context dominates over the raw event
patterns.fade_signal: Fade (buy after selloff) when: clearly strategic restructuring; strong balance sheet; management provides clear cost savings dollar amounts; sector is healthy overall
patterns.follow_signal: Follow (sell/avoid) when: layoffs accompanied by revenue guidance cut; language about 'demand softness' or 'macroeconomic headwinds'; repeated rounds of layoffs in 12-month period

context_matrix.restructuring_bull_market: +1% to +3% (efficiency story; market rewards cost discipline)
context_matrix.restructuring_bear_market: -1% to -3% (market focuses on what business conditions forced the action)
context_matrix.ai_related_2024_2026: 56% of AI layoff announcements traded negative from announcement; avg decline ~25%

patterns.notes: 2022-2023 tech layoffs were initially well-received because companies had massively over-hired in 2020-2021. That specific dynamic has largely normalized. Modern layoff reactions are more nuanced and require management commentary analysis.

patterns.name: Merger/Acquisition — Target Company
patterns.description: Company receives a formal acquisition offer. Target stock immediately gaps to near the offer price. Residual discount represents merger arbitrage spread (deal risk premium).
patterns.avg_return_1d: +20% to +35% (sustained; closes near offer price minus arb spread)
patterns.avg_return_7d: +19% to +33% (slight discount to offer maintained as deal risk premium)
patterns.avg_return_30d: +18% to +32% (convergence toward offer price if no deal issues)
patterns.typical_acquisition_premium_vs_unaffecte: 20-30% (normal range); up to 50% in competitive situations
patterns.confidence: HIGH — one of the most predictable single-event reactions in markets
patterns.fade_signal: Fade (sell after spike) when: regulatory approval risk is very high (antitrust in tech/telecom/pharma); stock already trading near or above offer price; deal structure is complex (stock-for-stock with acquirer risks)
patterns.follow_signal: Follow (buy on dip below offer) when: cash deal with strong acquirer balance sheet; regulatory path clear; target trading 5-10% below offer (pure arb capture)

arb_mechanics.cash_deal: Target trades 1-5% below offer price (deal risk premium); collapses to 0 on close
arb_mechanics.stock_deal: Target trades 3-8% below offer (plus acquirer stock fluctuation risk)
arb_mechanics.competitive_bid: Can trade above initial offer if second bidder expected; second bids average 10-15% above first
arb_mechanics.deal_break: If deal breaks: target retraces to pre-announcement price minus any permanent value revelation

patterns.notes: Run-up before announcement: M&A announcements are often preceded by stock price increases (information leaks). Most academic studies use '4 weeks before announcement' as unaffected price baseline. Do not use announcement day as baseline for calculating the premium.

patterns.name: Merger/Acquisition — Acquiring Company
patterns.description: Company announces it is acquiring another company. Markets typically punish acquirers immediately due to overpayment risk, integration risk, and potential balance sheet strain.
patterns.confidence: MODERATE — direction (negative) is reliable; magnitude varies widely
patterns.fade_signal: Fade (buy the acquirer dip) when: bolt-on acquisition; minimal premium (<20%); obvious synergies in core business; cash-financed with strong balance sheet; serial acquirer with track record
patterns.follow_signal: Follow (avoid acquirer) when: large transformative acquisition (>$1B for mid-cap); high premium (>40%); debt-financed deal materially changing credit profile; first large acquisition by management team
patterns.long_term_note: Academic consensus: majority of acquisitions fail to create value for acquiring shareholders over 3-5 year period. Day-1 market judgment (down) is historically more often right than wrong.

stock_vs_cash_deal.cash_deal: Acquirer typically down more (-3% to -5%) due to balance sheet concern and debt signal
stock_vs_cash_deal.stock_deal: Acquirer down less initially (-1% to -3%) but dilution risk priced over time
stock_vs_cash_deal.all_stock_deal_signal: Management using stock as currency often signals they believe stock is overvalued

patterns.notes: Exception sectors: pharmaceutical bolt-ons (pipeline diversification well-understood), software tuck-ins (ARR multiple math is clear to market). In these sectors, well-structured acquisitions may receive flat or positive day-1 reaction.

patterns.name: Federal Reserve Hawkish Surprise (More Aggressive Than Expected)
patterns.description: Fed delivers a more hawkish message than market consensus expected — either larger hike, faster pace of tightening, or aggressive language about rate path.
patterns.confidence: HIGH — direction reliable; magnitude depends on surprise magnitude
patterns.historical_example: Jackson Hole August 2022 (Powell hawkish pivot): S&P 500 -3.4% same day
patterns.fade_signal: Fade (buy after drop) when: hawkishness is purely verbal/communication; subsequent inflation data lower than expected; market had already priced aggressive tightening
patterns.follow_signal: Follow (stay bearish) when: inflation re-accelerating; Fed credibility under pressure; signals QT acceleration on top of hikes; financial conditions too loose

sector_impact.currencies: USD strengthens; EM currencies under pressure

patterns.notes: The 'hawkish cut' scenario is particularly important: when the Fed cuts rates but signals a slow future pace, the net reaction can be negative despite a nominal rate cut. Market trades the forward path, not the current action.

patterns.name: Federal Reserve Dovish Surprise (More Accommodative Than Expected)
patterns.description: Fed delivers a more dovish message than market consensus — smaller hike/cut than expected, slower tightening pace, or explicitly accommodative language.
patterns.confidence: HIGH — direction reliable; magnitude depends on macro confirmation
patterns.historical_example: Jackson Hole August 2025 (Powell dovish, rate cuts signaled): S&P 500 +1.3% same day; Dow +680 points (+1.5%)
patterns.fade_signal: Fade when: dovish because economy is terrible (recession signal masked as accommodation); inflation still clearly above target; dollar weakness signals stagflation concern
patterns.follow_signal: Follow when: inflation clearly under control; employment still healthy (soft landing); multiple future cuts signaled; 'insurance cut' language used
patterns.notes: Dovish surprises from a position of economic strength (rates being cut into solid growth) are more bullish than dovish surprises from economic weakness. The macro context behind the dovishness determines whether equities rally sustainably or just bounce temporarily.

patterns.name: Major Product Launch (Apple, Tesla, Major Tech)
patterns.description: Highly anticipated product announcement or launch event. Classic 'buy the rumor, sell the news' pattern where speculation premium unwinds on actual announcement.
patterns.avg_return_1h: Flat to -1.5% on launch day (if widely anticipated)
patterns.avg_return_30d: Returns to fundamental trajectory; actual sales data starts driving price
patterns.confidence: MODERATE — buy-rumor-sell-news is real but magnitude is hard to predict
patterns.pre_launch_pattern: Typical run-up: +5% to +15% in 30-60 days before major product events; creates the reversion setup
patterns.fade_signal: Fade (sell before/at launch) when: stock up >15% in prior 6 weeks; product features were pre-leaked; all analyst expectations already incorporated; historical pattern applies to this company
patterns.follow_signal: Follow (hold through launch) when: product is genuinely transformational and opens new market category; pre-launch setup was cautious (stock flat or down going in); company has consistently surprised to the upside on actual product performance

historical_exceptions.iphone_2007: Transformational; stock continued higher through and after launch
historical_exceptions.m1_chip_2020: Significant performance improvement; Apple stock continued to rise
historical_exceptions.cybertruck: Stock fell on reveal day; consistent with typical pattern for Tesla events

patterns.sector_applicability: Primarily consumer electronics, EVs, major software releases. Less applicable to B2B enterprise products (different timing/revenue recognition dynamics).
patterns.notes: The buy-rumor-sell-news pattern is self-reinforcing: as more traders expect the selloff and sell pre-launch, the run-up is shorter and the post-launch decline smaller. The pattern is best exploited in the lead-up (riding the rumor rally), not in trying to short the news day.

patterns.name: Geopolitical Shock (Wars, Attacks, Major Escalations)
patterns.description: Unexpected geopolitical events including military conflicts, terrorist attacks, major escalations. Historically, these produce sharp but temporary declines that represent buying opportunities in most cases.
patterns.avg_return_30d: Typically fully recovered; often positive vs. pre-shock price
patterns.avg_return_6m: +4.6% (average after major geopolitical shock, per JP Morgan data)
patterns.historical_recovery_rate: 19 of 20 major military conflicts since WWII: S&P 500 recovered within 28 days
patterns.confidence: HIGH (recovery pattern is extremely consistent); MODERATE (timing of bottom is uncertain)
patterns.fade_signal: Fade (buy the dip) when: no clear economic transmission mechanism (trade routes/commodity supply not disrupted); involves peripheral countries with limited global economic impact; initial selloff >5% without fundamental earnings impact
patterns.follow_signal: Follow (stay cautious) when: direct energy/commodity supply disruption (oil embargo-style); nuclear escalation risk; involves major economic powers directly (U.S./China/EU); sustained multi-year supply chain disruption
patterns.vix_behavior: VIX typically spikes to 25-40+ on geopolitical shock; provides additional buy signal when combined with historical recovery data
patterns.notes: Key distinction: 'pure' geopolitical shocks (limited economic transmission) vs. economic shocks that happen to be geopolitically caused. The 1973 oil embargo was fundamentally different from the Gulf War or 9/11 in economic impact. The more direct the economic channel, the longer and deeper the market decline.

patterns.name: FDA Drug Approval (Biotech/Pharma)
patterns.description: FDA approves a drug application (NDA/BLA). Magnitude depends heavily on company size, drug category, disease area, and degree of market anticipation.
patterns.avg_return_30d_small_biotech: Depends on reimbursement, launch, and commercial guidance
patterns.confidence: HIGH (direction for small biotech); MODERATE (magnitude varies by anticipation level)
patterns.fade_signal: Fade (sell after spike) when: approval was near-100% expected by options market; approved label is narrower than expected; reimbursement/pricing risk remains significant; stock had already run 50-100% pre-approval
patterns.follow_signal: Follow when: surprise approval (market implied probability was <70%); first-in-class drug; large unmet medical need market; breakthrough therapy or fast-track designation granted; no competitor approvals imminent
patterns.notes: Company classification (early biotech vs. large pharma) is the single biggest driver of magnitude. Disease category, trial phase, and competitive landscape also matter. Rare disease approvals (small patient populations with no alternatives) tend to produce larger % moves than crowded therapeutic areas.

patterns.name: FDA Drug Rejection (Complete Response Letter — CRL)
patterns.description: FDA issues a Complete Response Letter (CRL) declining to approve a drug application. Among the most severe single-event stock declines for small biotech companies.
patterns.avg_return_30d_small_biotech: Often -50% to -90% for single-asset companies; may stabilize if other pipeline
patterns.asymmetry_vs_approval: Rejection magnitude EXCEEDS approval magnitude; downside persists longer than upside (academic finding: confirmed in ResearchGate biotech study)
patterns.confidence: HIGH — rejection is one of the most predictable catastrophic single-event declines
patterns.fade_signal: Fade (cautious buy) ONLY when: CRL was for manufacturing/CMC issues only (fixable; not efficacy/safety); company explicitly states refiling timeline; company has other pipeline assets that weren't affected; stock has already fallen 70%+ (terminal value floor)
patterns.follow_signal: Follow (avoid the name) when: safety rejection or clinical hold; single-asset company with no other pipeline; management communication is vague about path forward; FDA raises efficacy questions (not just manufacturing)

crl_type_matters.manufacturing_cmc_issue: Fixable; stock may recover 30-50% of loss over 6-12 months post-resubmission
crl_type_matters.safety_issue: Near-terminal for the specific indication; rarely reversed
crl_type_matters.efficacy_issue: Program likely dead; requires entirely new trials
crl_type_matters.labeling_issue: Moderate; negotiation possible; partial recovery common

patterns.notes: Never 'catch a falling knife' on FDA rejection day. Wait for management conference call to understand the nature of the rejection. Manufacturing/CMC rejections are recoverable; safety rejections are generally not. The CRL letter text is critical information.
